questions.question: In JSX, why must your own components use PascalCase (e.g. `<Button>`) rather than a lowercase name?
options: JSX treats a lowercase tag as a built-in DOM element (the string `"button"`) and a Capitalized tag as a reference to your component
Lowercase component names cause a runtime error because React reserves all lowercase identifiers
PascalCase is only a style convention enforced by ESLint; either case renders identically
Lowercase tags are rendered but stripped of all props by the compiler
questions.explanation: The reviewer states a lowercase tag is emitted as a DOM element (string `"div"`/`"button"`) while a Capitalized tag is a component reference. It is a real semantic rule of the JSX transform, not merely a lint convention.

questions.question: What does a JSX tag actually compile to under a modern toolchain (Babel/SWC)?
options: A call to the JSX runtime (`jsx`/`jsxs`) that returns a plain React element object `{ type, props, key, ... }`
An HTML string that is injected into the DOM via `innerHTML`
A template that React re-parses at runtime on every render
A direct DOM node created with `document.createElement`
questions.explanation: The reviewer describes JSX as sugar for object creation: the compiler turns each tag into a `jsx`/`jsxs` call (the modern replacement for `React.createElement`) returning a plain element object, which the reconciler later turns into DOM. It is not a template engine and produces no HTML string.

questions.question: Which set of JSX attribute names is correct?
options: `className`, `htmlFor`, `onClick`, `tabIndex`
`class`, `for`, `onclick`, `tabindex`
`className`, `for`, `onClick`, `tab-index`
`class`, `htmlFor`, `onclick`, `tabIndex`
questions.explanation: JSX uses camelCase props that map to DOM properties (`onClick`, `tabIndex`) and renames the JS reserved words: `className` (not `class`) and `htmlFor` (not `for`). The other options mix in HTML-style lowercase or reserved-word names.

questions.question: Content nested between a component's opening and closing tags is delivered to that component as `props.children`.
questions.explanation: The reviewer states `children` is just a prop: content nested between tags arrives as `props.children`, which is what enables wrapper/layout components.

questions.question: The "double curlies" in `style={{ marginTop: 8 }}` are best explained as:
options: The outer `{}` enters JS and the inner `{}` is an ordinary object literal — `style` takes an object with camelCased CSS keys
Special two-brace syntax that React added specifically for CSS
A way to escape a string so the CSS is passed through verbatim
A template literal that interpolates CSS at build time
questions.explanation: Per the reviewer, `{{ }}` is not special syntax: the outer braces enter JS and the inner braces form an object literal. `style` requires an object whose CSS keys are camelCased (e.g. `marginTop`).

questions.question:
A developer writes `

{ if (loggedIn) { name } }

` and it fails to compile. Why?
options: Curly braces in JSX embed an expression, not a statement; `if` is a statement, so use a ternary or `&&` instead
JSX forbids reading variables like `name` inside braces; only literals are allowed

The `

` element cannot contain dynamic text in JSX
`if` must be written as `If` (PascalCase) to be recognized inside JSX
questions.explanation: The reviewer notes `{expr}` interpolates any JS expression but statements like `if`/`for` are not allowed inside JSX braces; you express conditionals with `&&` or a ternary. Variables and function calls are perfectly fine inside braces.

questions.question: You need a component to return two sibling `<tr>` rows directly inside a `<tbody>`, without introducing an extra wrapper element in the DOM. What should you use?
options: A Fragment (`<>...</>` or `<Fragment>`), which groups siblings without adding a DOM node
A `<div>` wrapper, since every component must return a real DOM element
Return an array literal directly with no keys and no wrapper
Two separate `return` statements, one per row
questions.explanation: A Fragment satisfies the single-root rule while adding no extra DOM node — exactly the case the reviewer cites for table rows. A wrapping `<div>` would inject an invalid node into `<tbody>`, and a function cannot have two reachable `return` statements producing both rows.

questions.question: Inside a `.map()` you group a `<dt>`/`<dd>` pair per item and need to attach a `key`. Which form is required?
options: The explicit `<Fragment key={i.id}>` imported from `react`
The shorthand `<key={i.id}>...</>`, which accepts a key
Any shorthand `<>...</>`, since React derives keys from list position automatically
A `<div key={i.id}>`, because only DOM elements can carry a key
questions.explanation: The shorthand `<>` cannot take a `key` or any attribute, so for keyed grouping inside a map you must use the explicit `<Fragment key={...}>` form imported from `react`. A `<div>` would work syntactically but reintroduces the wrapper node the Fragment exists to avoid.

questions.question: What is the precise meaning of "a component must be pure during render"?
options: Given the same props, state, and context, the component returns identical JSX with no observable side effects while React computes the UI
The component must avoid using props and rely only on local constants
The component must never trigger side effects anywhere in the application
The component must be wrapped in `React.memo` so it never re-renders
questions.explanation: Purity means same inputs (props/state/context) produce the same output JSX with no side effects during the render pass. It does not ban side effects from the app entirely — it relocates them to event handlers and effects — and it has nothing to do with `React.memo`.

questions.question: Creating an array inside a component's render and pushing items into it during that same render violates purity.
questions.explanation: The reviewer explicitly allows local mutation: an object/array created inside this render never existed before, so nothing else can observe the mutation. Purity is only violated by mutating things that existed before the render (props, prior state, module-level vars).

questions.question: A value can be computed entirely from existing props and state. Where should it live?
options: Computed inline during render (optionally with `useMemo`), not mirrored into state via an effect
Stored in state and kept in sync with a `useEffect`
Fetched again from the server on each render to stay authoritative
Written to a module-level variable so all renders can read it
questions.explanation: The reviewer says derived data is not an effect: if a value is computable from existing props/state, compute it during render (optionally `useMemo`) rather than mirroring it into state through an effect. The other options add needless state, fetches, or shared mutable globals.

questions.question: Which statements about `<StrictMode>` are correct?
options: In development it double-invokes render to surface accidental impurity
It re-runs effect setup→cleanup→setup so non-idempotent effects show problems
It is a dev-only check; production renders once
It permanently changes production behavior so renders always run twice
It memoizes components so unchanged renders are skipped
questions.explanation: The reviewer says StrictMode double-invokes render and re-runs effects (setup→cleanup→setup) in development to catch impurity, and is dev-only with production rendering once. It does not alter production behavior and is unrelated to memoization.

questions.question: Why does React rely on components being pure?
options: Purity lets React skip, reorder, restart, or concurrently run renders — the foundation for transitions and Suspense streaming
Purity makes the JSX compiler emit smaller bundles
Purity guarantees components never need to re-render after the first paint
Purity removes the need for keys when rendering lists
questions.explanation: The reviewer ties purity to React's ability to memoize/bail out, reorder or restart work, and render concurrently — enabling transitions and Suspense streaming. An impure render makes those optimizations produce wrong UI. It has nothing to do with bundle size or list keys.

questions.question: Consider this component. What is the core problem, and what does StrictMode reveal in development?
options: It mutates a module-level variable that existed before this render; StrictMode double-invokes render so the displayed count is wrong
It is pure but slow; StrictMode warns about the performance of the increment
The `<h2>` cannot interpolate a number; StrictMode flags the type error
`guestCount` should be `const`; StrictMode rewrites it automatically at runtime
questions.explanation: Incrementing `guestCount` mutates a variable that existed before the render — a side effect during render. Because StrictMode double-invokes render in dev, the count is incremented twice per render, producing visibly wrong numbers. JSX interpolates numbers fine, and StrictMode neither measures performance nor rewrites code.

questions.question: Which of the following, if done during render, violate purity by mutating something that existed before this render?
options: Reassigning a field on the incoming `props` object
Pushing into an array you received via a captured closure from an earlier render
Writing to a `ref.current` you did not create during this render
Pushing into an array you declared with `const` at the top of this render's body
Returning a different object literal each render computed from props
questions.explanation: Mutating props, a closure captured from a previous render, or a pre-existing `ref.current` all touch state that existed before this render — forbidden. Creating and mutating a fresh local array during this render is fine, and returning a freshly computed object derived from props is the normal pure case.

questions.question: A network request must be sent in direct response to a user clicking "Save". According to the guidance, where does this belong?
options: In the event handler, the default home for effects caused by a user action
In a `useEffect`, because all side effects must go through effects
Inline in the render body, since the data depends on current state
In a module-level function called during render so it runs once
questions.explanation: The reviewer designates event handlers as the home for side effects caused by a user action (click/submit/type); they run during the interaction and may call APIs. `useEffect` is for synchronizing with external systems when no event triggered the work — not the right tool for a click-driven request — and side effects never belong in the render body.

questions.question: The reviewer says effects "must be safe to re-run (setup→cleanup→setup) and must return cleanup." What does this imply for a `useEffect` that opens a subscription?
options: It must return a cleanup that tears down the subscription, so a re-run does not leak or stack duplicate subscriptions
It should open the subscription only on the very first mount and never clean it up
It must call `setState` synchronously so React knows the subscription is active
It should be replaced by an event handler because subscriptions are user actions
questions.explanation: Effects synchronize with external systems and run setup→cleanup→setup, so the subscription effect must return cleanup that tears it down; otherwise re-runs leak or stack duplicates. Subscriptions are external-system sync (effects), not user-triggered actions, and there is no requirement to call `setState` synchronously.

questions.question: This component fails to compile. What is the precise reason, and the minimal fix?
options: JSX must return a single root node; wrap the two siblings in a parent element or a Fragment (`<>...</>`)

The `

` tag is not self-closed; change it to `

`
`return` cannot span multiple lines in JSX; put everything on one line
Text content must be wrapped in `{}`; change the strings to `{"Welcome"}` and `{"Thanks for visiting"}`
questions.explanation:
A component returns one node, so two adjacent siblings are invalid; wrapping them in a parent or a Fragment fixes it. Self-closing applies to void elements (`
`/``), not `

` with children; multi-line `return` is allowed when properly parenthesized, and plain text needs no braces.
